plainMeaning: Reviewing the seventh vow — the one limiting what I eat and how I earn a living — I confess any slips in its five food-rules and in the fifteen harmful trades I have done or condoned today.
recitation: e sātamā bhogopabhoga-vratataṇā pāṁca atiyāra śodhuṁ. sacitta āhāre, sacitta paḍibaddha āhāre. appoṣahi bhakkhaṇayā, dupposahi bhakkhaṇayā, tucchosahi bhakkhaṇayā. e pāṁca atiyāra māṁhe. || karmataḥ pannare karmādāna — iṅgāla-kamme, vaṇa-kamme, sāḍī-kamme, bhāḍī-kamme, phoḍī-kamme, danta vāṇijje, lakkha vāṇijje, rasa vāṇijje, visa vāṇijje, kesa vāṇijje, jantapīlaṇa kamme, nilañchaṇa kamme, davaggi dāvaṇayā, sara daha talāva sosaṇayā, asaï posaṇayā — e pannara karmādāna viṣe sthūla niyama, sūkṣmataṇī jayaṇā. e pannara karmādāna māṁhe.

wordByWord.gloss: this, these (pointing to the vow just named)
wordByWord.notes: Opens the standard confession formula.
Marks vow 7 of the twelve.

wordByWord.gloss: of the vow limiting consumables and re-usables
wordByWord.etymology: bhoga (once-used enjoyment, e.g. food) + upabhoga (repeatedly-used enjoyment, e.g. clothes, furniture) + vrata + Gujarati genitive ‘-taṇā’ (= ‘of’)
wordByWord.notes: ‘taṇā’ is the recurring possessive seen in every vow’s formula.
Glossary term; spelt with ય per this edition.
‘I review-and-purify’; sets up the self-examination.
Raw, un-rendered plant matter still holding one-sensed life.

wordByWord.gloss: eating food joined to sentient matter (e.g. fruit still on stone/stalk) — 2nd atichāra
wordByWord.notes: The edible part is acitta but stuck to a living part.
auṣadhi here = vegetable/grain foodstuff, not medicine.
Improper cooking leaves living organisms intact.

wordByWord.gloss: eating trifling plant-food (much destroyed, little edible) — 5th atichāra
wordByWord.notes: e.g. tender pods that kill many lives for scant nourishment.

wordByWord.gloss: among / within (i.e. ‘in these I confess whatever I committed’)
wordByWord.notes: The closing tag of each atichāra block; the day/night confession-refrain is understood.
Marks the shift from the food-side to the occupation-side.

wordByWord.gloss: as to occupation / by way of livelihood
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit ablative karma + -taḥ (‘from the standpoint of work’)
wordByWord.notes: Pairs with the ‘bhojanataḥ’ (food-side) named in the previous verse.
Glossary term; the fifteen are now enumerated.
Occupations that consume fire-bodies and scorch life.
Destruction of plant-bodied beings.
Standard list reads ‘śakaṭa-karma’.
Profit from overloading carts and draught animals.
Mining and earth-rending harm earth-bodies.

wordByWord.gloss: 6. trade in ivory, tusk, bone, animal parts
wordByWord.notes: Begins the five vāṇijya (commerce) items.

wordByWord.gloss: 7. trade in lac/shellac (and resins killing insects)
8. trade in liquids/intoxicants — liquor, honey, fat, butter
wordByWord.notes: ‘Rasa’ covers fermented and animal fluids.

wordByWord.gloss: 9. trade in poison, weapons, and lethal goods
wordByWord.notes: Includes arms and toxic implements.

wordByWord.gloss: 10. trade in hair / hairy livestock (and bonded beings)
wordByWord.notes: Traditionally extended to traffic in animals and people.
Pressing pulps countless small lives.
Not ‘de-husking’; it is bodily mutilation of cattle.

wordByWord.etymology: davāgni (dava ‘wildfire’ + agni ‘fire’) + dāpana (‘causing to be given/lit’)
wordByWord.notes: Mass destruction of mobile and immobile life.
Kills water-bodied and aquatic beings.

wordByWord.gloss: 15. rearing/keeping (animals or people) for immoral gain
wordByWord.notes: Breeding for sale and keeping beings for vice.
Qualifies the firm rule the householder can keep outright.

wordByWord.gloss: firm rule (here: total abstention at the gross level)
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term, applied here to the karmādāna.
Pairs by contrast with ‘sthūla’.

wordByWord.gloss: prudent care/leeway (where total avoidance is impossible)
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term; the niyama–jayaṇā pairing is the verse’s pivot.
Seals the karmādāna block as the food-block was sealed.

literalTranslation: I scrutinise the five transgressions of this seventh bhogopabhoga vow: eating sentient food; eating food bound to sentient matter; eating unripe plant-food; eating ill-cooked plant-food; eating trifling plant-food. Among these five (I confess whatever I did). || As to livelihood, the fifteen karmādāna: charcoal-work, forest-work, cart-work, hire-work, quarry-work; ivory-trade, lac-trade, fluid-trade, poison-trade, hair-trade; mill-crushing-work, mutilation-work, forest-firing, draining of lake-pool-tank, rearing-for-vice. Regarding these fifteen karmādāna — a firm rule at the gross level, careful caution as to the subtle. Among these fifteen (I confess whatever I did).
idiomaticTranslation: Now I examine the seventh vow, by which I limit both what I consume and how I earn. On the side of food, I confess its five slips: taking food still alive with one-sensed beings; food clinging to a living part; food unripe, food half-cooked, or food so insubstantial that many lives are destroyed for a morsel. For all such lapses today — may they be undone. And on the side of livelihood, I weigh myself against the fifteen ruinous trades: the fire-trades and forest-felling; cart-making and beast-hire; quarrying; the commerce in ivory, lac, strong liquids, poison and hair; the crushing-mills, the gelding of cattle, the lighting of wildfires, the draining of lakes and ponds, and the keeping of creatures for vice. Where I can, I bind myself by firm rule outright; where the harm is too subtle to escape entirely, I move with vigilant care. For whatever I transgressed among these fifteen — may my wrongdoing be in vain.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse completes the seventh vow that the previous line only opened. There the vow was declared ‘two-fold — by food (bhojanataḥ) and by occupation (karmataḥ)’; here the reciter discharges both halves in sequence, and the single word ‘karmataḥ’ is the hinge that swings the confession from the plate to the workshop. On the food side, notice that the five slips are not about *what* is forbidden but about *carelessness*: the disciplined layperson already renders food acitta, so the lapses catalogue the ways rendering fails — eating something still alive (sacitta), something fused to a living part (sacitta-paḍibaddha), or something unripe, ill-cooked, or so meagre that the kill-to-nourishment ratio is shameful (the three auṣadhi-bhakṣaṇa terms). The recension’s ‘appoṣahi / dupposahi / tucchosahi’ preserves the older auṣadhi (‘herb-food’) wording where the Tattvārtha recension reads ‘sammiśra-abhiṣava-duṣpakva’. The occupation side then unfurls the fifteen karmādāna in their classic three sub-groups — five *karma* (fire, forest, cart, hire, quarry), five *vāṇijya* (ivory, lac, fluids, poison, hair), and five residual cruelties (mill-pressing, gelding, arson, draining waters, breeding for vice). What is genuinely verse-specific, and worth pausing on, is the closing clause ‘sthūla niyama, sūkṣmataṇī jayaṇā.’ The householder cannot truthfully claim to have escaped every karmic ripple of commerce, so the vow is calibrated: an outright **firm rule** at the gross, visible level — *I will not deal in poison, ivory, arson* — paired with **vigilant care** at the subtle level, where supply-chains and incidental harm cannot be wholly severed. That honest two-tier standard, rather than a false claim of total purity, is exactly what the confession then asks pardon for falling short of.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse operationalises ahiṁsā as economics. Where the food-atichāras guard the one-sensed life in what enters the body, the karmādāna guard against livelihoods whose very structure manufactures violence — and the tradition rates them as especially binding because the harm is *systemic and ongoing*, not a single act. The niyama–jayaṇā pairing encodes the layperson’s defining status as *viratāvirata* (‘restrained-yet-unrestrained’): unlike the muni’s total renunciation, the householder’s vow is partial by design, firm where avoidance is possible and merely careful where it is not. Confessing the gap between that ideal and the day’s conduct is the whole point of the atichāra review.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: A reciter today reads the fifteen not as a quaint medieval list but as a livelihood-audit: arms and poison trades, deforestation, factory-scale animal breeding, and industries built on cruelty map onto the old categories directly. The verse asks two honest questions each evening — ‘Was I careless about the life in my food?’ and ‘Does my work, or my investment, sit among these fifteen?’ — and offers a workable ethic: draw a hard line where you can (sthūla niyama), and where modern supply-chains make total avoidance impossible, act with deliberate, minimising care (jayaṇā) rather than pretending innocence.

crossReferences.text: Upāsakadaśāḥ-sūtra — Ānanda’s vow and the enumeration of the fifteen karmādāna
Yogaśāstra 3.99–113 (Hemacandra) — the fifteen karmādāna under the bhogopabhoga guṇavrata

sources.title: Tattvārtha-sūtra, Chapter 7 (vows and their transgressions; sūtra 7.35 on bhogopabhoga atichāras)
sources.consultedFor: Doctrinal framework of the householder’s vows and the canonical five atichāras of the consumption-limiting vow.

sources.title: Anuvrata / Śrāvaka vows (Wikipedia)
sources.consultedFor: Placement of bhogopabhoga-parimāṇa among the three guṇavratas and the viratāvirata status of the layperson.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim verse text, the ‘atiyāra’ orthography, and the recension’s appoṣahi/dupposahi/tucchosahi auṣadhi-bhakṣaṇa wording.

sources.title: Jain eLibrary (jainqq.org) — śrāvakācāra references for the fifteen karmādāna
sources.consultedFor: Cross-checking the standard three-fold grouping (5 karma + 5 vāṇijya + 5 sāmānya) and individual karmādāna glosses.

notes: The atichāra terms appoṣahi/dupposahi/tucchosahi are this recension's Prakrit forms of apakva-auṣadhi / duṣpakva-auṣadhi / tuccha-auṣadhi bhakṣaṇa; here ‘auṣadhi’ means plant/herb FOODSTUFF, not medicine (some popular glosses mistranslate it). The Tattvārtha (Digambara) recension lists the same vow's atichāras differently as sacitta-sambandha-sammiśra-abhiṣava-duṣpakva (7.35); the food-set is doctrinally equivalent but not verbatim identical — preserved, not normalised. Among the fifteen, ‘nilañchaṇa’ is castration/branding/mutilation of animals (not de-husking grain), and ‘asaï-posaṇa’ is rearing beings for immoral gain (asatī-poṣaṇa); these are the standard scholarly readings. ‘sāḍī-kamme’ = śakaṭa-karma (carts/conveyances) and ‘bhāḍī-kamme’ = bhāṭaka (hire/transport for fare) per the classical list. The verse spans BOTH halves of vow 7 (bhojanataḥ five atichāras + karmataḥ fifteen karmādāna), the food-half having been introduced in the preceding verse; this is structural, not repetition. ‘॥’ retained verbatim as book punctuation.
